speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. This is tape four. I am conducting an interview with Eva Schutz. The date is October 31, 1996. The location, London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maryla kept me for a few days and found me a job as a shoemaker with a friend of hers who was also a doctor's wife. Her-- her husband was also in the army, and she made boots out of felt. They were special Polish boots called [POLISH]. I had no idea of shoe making, but I learned it. But in the meantime, Jan was at the Mariani, and Marisha let me know that he had to be moved somehow, but she will arrange that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Mariani didn't-- couldn't keep him. He was, I think, also a bit of a different child of the others. He was stubborn, and not-- not easy, I suppose. But he had a very hard life, and I can't blame him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So perhaps he didn't get on with the other children. He found perhaps that he's better, or more educated, or what. I don't know who the other children were. Were some of them-- were any of them Jewish, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the other children Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there certainly were Jewish children, but there were a lot of not Jewish children, but Polish children. And the Marianas had enough on their, and since they were not very helpful, Maryla had to move Jan somewhere. And she found a place in the county for him at a Polish gentleman who was very anti-German, naturally. And when he heard there's a child of a Polish officer whose mother is missing, or perhaps, dead-- not that he knew that he is Jewish, but a lot of Poles were taken away and killed, or whatever, arrested, and nobody heard anything about them. Auschwitz, and-- and Dachau, and Bergen-Belsen was full of Polish people also. So anyway, he agreed to have Jan, and Marisha paid for that. Not much, but something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somewhere near Warsaw, in the country. Jan didn't like being there, because this engineer wasn't a very nice man. But he had to stay there, and I couldn't do anything about it. I had no other place for him. In the meantime, I had no place to stay. One of the ladies in the-- in the work home gave me-- for a few days, a bed. Somebody else gave me a bed, but I remember that sometimes when it was warm, I was sleeping in the park, or somewhere outside. And that was-- that was the problem then. My things, whatever I had, were in the wash in the work home, whatever I had extra dress or some shoes or something. Then one of the ladies who worked in the shoe-- shoe-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was also a shop. It was not only a work home, but she worked in the work room-- knew that I am homeless. And she suggested me going to a nunnery and asking one of the nuns whether they would perhaps know somebody who could give me a room, or perhaps I could work for somebody. Somebody would have needed somebody to look after a child or an old woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is as a Polish woman that you are now on the false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, naturally I was a Polish woman. Nobody knew that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Could you tell us the name that you had on your false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I was Eva Sarnetzka. And Jan was Jan Sarnetzky. We kept the initials so it will be E-S and G-S, in case something would be found on us with a monogram on it. We had to think of everything. Well anyway, I went as I was there as Eva Sarnetzka. I had the identity card. I had my baptism paper. And I went to this nunnery in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the place were I lived in for a few years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw Sister Laurentia was her name. She was an elderly nun. Well, for me she was elderly, because I was young. And she was very nice to me. I told her the same story. Everybody I met, which was true. A captain's wife. He's in the army. I don't know where he is. I have nowhere to go. I work. I am a shoemaker now, and could she know somebody who could give me a home?

speaker: interviewee
text: She thought a long time and asked a few questions, nothing to do with Jewish. I don't think she knew that I was Jewish. And then she said, yes, she thinks she knows somebody who may have a-- a-- a room, because the husband of this lady is also in the army, is also abroad somewhere. And they had quite a nice house. There are two girls who go to this-- they are at the nunnery at the school, not a boarding. Not boarding, but they come every day. And Mrs. Stelmachowska may know, if she hasn't got a room, she may know somebody, because it was the officers colony there, where she lived. There were only officers' houses. So I went there and Mrs. Stelmachowska let me in, and we were talking. And I told her that, again, the same story. My husband is in the army, he left. And I didn't tell her then that I have a child. I work as a shoemaker, and I hope I will see my husband after the war, and so on. Well, all the-- the things. And Mrs. Stelmachowska said, she has a very small room. It was the servant's room, because they had, naturally, a servant. He was a colonel in the army. They were not rich. They were not wealthy, but they were well off. And a very nice house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if that's good enough for me, yes, she will let me have it. And I said, but I can't pay much. She said that doesn't matter, really. What can I pay? I told her what I can pay, very little.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know that if I would have said I have thousands of zlotas or whatever the money was, I can't remember, she would have been very suspicious, because no ordinary person would pay for a room thousands. There must be something wrong with this woman. So she said, all right, and I was over the moon. Next, I think I stayed overnight there in this room. She had linen and everything. And next day I came to the work room. I thanked her. Oh, I went in the morning to Sister Laurentia, I think, and thanked her. Told her-- or the same evening. I don't know, it's all gone for me. But next day, I told this lady in the work room that I got a room now, and it will be much better now. I will be much calmer. I had my ration card, as everybody else. Not that we could buy much. Some so-called jam that was beet roots and apples, and some bread. Well, that was sand, more or less. Some margarine, queues everywhere. But I managed, somehow. I cooked a little bit there. I got something from Mrs. Stelmachowska. I met the two girls. Very nice girls. We got on very well together. And after a few days, in the evening when we were sitting, perhaps we had a cup of so-called tea, I told Mrs. Stelmachowska that I feel very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't feel well, because I lied to you. I am Jewish. And I have a child, also. But it is true that my husband is in the Polish army. He was a doctor and all that, which was true. And Mrs. Stelmachowska looked at me, smiled, and said, but I know. We were then still on names Mrs. Stelmachowska, Mrs. Sarnetzka, but then later became her name was Irina, and I was Eva. So that was-- I know that you are Jewish. Don't bother about that. It's no difference. My daughters won't know that. If they know it, so well, they would keep it for themself. Later I know why they-- why they were not bothered that I am Jewish. They had more to hide than a Jewish woman. Well, I will tell you later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So life went on for me. I went every day by tram to work. Sometimes I visited Milek and Lusia who lived-- were in hiding. They were really in hiding. Not Lusia, but Milek never got out, never went out. He was-- he was helped by this Rozyca, who was his friend. He arranged for him to get two rooms with a very well known actress who was working in the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they stayed there. I visited them sometimes. I wrote them always news, because lUSIA looked Jewish, but she had papers as an Armian, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you repeat that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think an-- a Muslim woman. Armian, I think you called it, don't you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Armenian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Armenian. Yes. She had a passport as an Armenian woman, because she looked it. So she went out from time to time, but not much. They had money, because Lusia had a lot of jewelry. So that was sold. And don't forget that my jewelry was left in Warsaw with all my-- with our other things. I had-- perhaps I had pearls, which I gave to somebody for something. I can't remember. I had my-- still my wedding ring and my engagement ring, which was taken off by Germans in Luevstill, in the ghetto, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I came back to ghetto now. But I am working to work room, visiting Lusia and Milek sometimes. Sometimes I heard through Maryla about Jan, and now Mrs. Stelmachowska knew that I have a son. And she and a lady who was called-- she was the-- a doctor's wife. Her husband died in Russia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She-- they arranged for Jan to be taken into a boarding school for service men's children. So Jan was brought by somebody, I don't know by whom, to this school, which was in Warsaw. Not that-- I never went to visit him. I never showed my face. But it was-- I was really in heaven, then, with seeing all this misery around. And in the meantime there was the ghetto uprising. And my way to work was along the ghetto wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I heard the bombing, the shooting, the crying. I saw the flames. I saw people escaping and being just beaten to death by Germans when they found somebody escaping from there. I once saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the tram just stopped when somebody opened the water-- what you call it, where you go into the sewers? The storm. And somebody wanted to get out. There were Jews in the sewers. They wanted to get away from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a German soldier opened it completely and brought this man up. And just-- I don't know what happened. Either he shoot him, or whatever. But that was one of the sights I saw, amongst others, going to work every day. Well, the ghetto uprising was squashed, as everybody knows. The ghetto was completely demolished. All my family, nearly all my family, were in the ghetto, and-- and they're dead. Very few escaped. A few escaped. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then, well, life went on. But after this [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after this event that Jan was at school, I was much more composed and looked at it all that much calmer. I wasn't so nervous anymore. Mrs-- Mrs. Stelmachowska helped me a lot in that, I must say. Then I was a few times caught by Germans. They just closed a-- a road, or they stopped a tram, and everybody out. And well, I was lucky once in the tram, because I didn't go out, because this German said that there was a nun in it, and a-- a postman, and the conductor, and they can stay. And I sat also down. I was just going out, but I sat down. And then the German told the driver to get on, so we drove off. I was in there. And I heard just the shooting of all these people who was-- that was one of those nice things. I had another one. We were caught and put against a wall, and we had to count. One, two. One, two. One, two. One, two. I didn't know what it is for. Then this German-- there was a few-- said, all twos, out. Run. So we ran. And I just heard the shooting of all the other ones were there. There were a few of those episodes which I can't even remember. Perhaps I would remember if I would really think of it, but there is enough, I think, to give you a bit of a picture of it. I helped Mrs. Stelmachowska with some work with children. She had-- she worked in the-- in the office, the gasworks, and-- but she worked for Polish children, and for-- there was a nursery where the children were looked after when their parents were either taken away, or worked, or whatever. And they needed cotton wool, and they needed some ordinary bandages, or vitamins. And I i was-- I suppose I was silly, because it was just bravery and nothing more. I went into the German pharmacist which was just opposite where Lusia and Milek lived, and Milek saw me once going in. And he had a weak heart. As you know, he was ill before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he saw me going in, and he couldn't understand it. But he saw me coming out again with a parcel, and next time when he saw me, he asked, for heaven's sake, what did you do in the German pharmacy? And I said, well, I go there from time to time to get some cotton wool or bandages which Mrs. Stelmachowska needs for her nurseries.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was aghast. He said, never, never do that again. But they saw me, and I spoke good German. And so I said, I would like to have some cotton wool and some elastoplast, or what-- little things. Once they asked me whether I have a identity card. Naturally, they thought a German would have identity card. I said, yes. I have a identity card. But I didn't have one. So if they would have found that out, that I am Polish-- well, I'm not talking about Jewish, but Polish-- I wouldn't have come out of this. So I had to promise Milek that I never will do that again. I can't tell much more about that. No, I can say more about it, because that is what Mrs. Stelmachowska did for me, again. And Jan. She decided that Jan should come on Sunday for a meal to get off this, because the children there, in the school, were not really up to the standard of Jan out there. Even I must say that. What I heard about them, only what Jan said. I never saw them. But they were, well, Polish children from Polish-- they were not all officers' children. Could have been anybody. So Jan-- somebody brought Jan to Mrs. Stelmachowska.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never showed my face there, and Mrs. Stelmachowska didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they travel from his school?

speaker: interviewee
text: For years. Back tram. He was a Polish boy. He had his school legitimation. I showed you the legitimation.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- the first time, somebody brought him, because Mrs. Stelmachowska spoke to the head mistress or head master, I don't know. I was not involved in that. I never showed my face. I don't know whether Jan said that he has-- I think it was agreed to say that his mother is dead, that he's an-- the father is in the service, but the mother, no. He doesn't know. So he came. And, well, he was very happy. He made at once friends with Wisza and Ola. Wisza was much older. Wisza was seven years older. Ola is Jan's age.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he got pancakes. I remember this day when he came, Mrs. Stelmachowska made pancakes. And Mrs. Stelmachowska had some sardines, because she got from her husband, through the Polish Red Cross from Sweden, through Sweden, or through Portugal, very good food parcels, which she-- she nearly sold everything to buy ordinary things. There were also beautiful sweaters, which she kept for the girls. They were-- twice there were watches. But there were sardines. There were figs, which is sold. There were wonderful things. But she shared it then with me. And when Jan came, Jan always got a good meal.

speaker: interviewee
text: So slowly, slowly, we came-- no. It was not slowly, because at Easter, '44, we heard during the night German boots. You could hear them very well, even when you were fast asleep. It was Easter, and Jan was with us, because it was Easter holiday. And when it got light, we saw all the houses were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were closed by German soldiers, Gestapo, SS, you name it, they were there. With rifles, with what is [NON-ENGLISH] in English? I don't-- Machine gun, could it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Machine gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Machine guns. They had it all. And now we spoke-- I spoke to-- with Mrs. Stelmachowska what to do? We knew that they will come into the house. So first thing was that Jan was put into Ola's bed. The two children were together, and we still laughed at Jan and-- and Ola slept together when they were 13. When it got light, we dressed, Mrs. Stelmachowska and I, and starting cleaning the house. It was Easter. You had to clean the house. Oh, the German came. Well, they didn't knock. They just bumped. Mrs. Stelmachowska opened. She spoke good German also. I think she came from a German family but far, far aways, long ago. And she was very anti-German, certainly. But she spoke a good German and a good French, I think. So well, the German wanted [NON-ENGLISH], that is legitimations. Well, we showed our legitimations, and the children. Wisza was not at home. Wisza was in the country. It was holidays, it was Easter. She went to her godmother, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mrs. Stelmachowska showed the two children's legitimation, and showed that they are asleep in bed, and hers and mine. She worked at the gas work, and I worked as a shoemaker. Well, he wasn't very pleased with it. He went through the house. Looked everywhere. Didn't find any special thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so-- and but we went on with our work. Let him get on with it. We were not afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she took the carpets out, put them on the thing, and started cleaning the carpets. And I took a bag. And when I wanted to get out of the house, the German said, no, where are you going? You stay here. And I said, it is Good Friday and we had to bake something. There are two children in the house, and it is Easter. So I want to get a half a ration card, and I should get some flour, and some jam, and we want to make a cake, an [NON-ENGLISH], you know. But I had not in my mind. But I wanted to show that I am not afraid of him, that there's nothing to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he let me out. And I went off. I didn't go to a shop. I didn't have anything. I just went somewhere around. I was not afraid to go nowhere around. I had my-- my identity card. Oh, they left us in peace. The evening we heard-- it was the whole day going on. The-- the children went up and played in the garden. It was no school time, so they had not to go to school. And I did something in the house. And Mrs. Stelmachowska cleaned. In the evening, we heard that thousands and thousands of Jews were taken from [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was a good hiding place for Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were usually blackmailed. And then, after they had nothing more to pay or had nothing more to give, they were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] denounced. No, what? Denounced to Germans. So-- and then that was-- a lot of Poles did that, and Jews did that. Mrs. Stemahovksa certainly did not. So after that, life went on as usually, with all the terrible things which happened. We knew, because Mrs. Stelmachowska had a radio-- I never listened to it-- but she knew that the Germans are on the-- on the go. Every time it was, they took on new positions, they strengthened their positions. And we knew what that means. That means back, back, back.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was with my-- what my mother used to say. Hitler will swallow too much. He will have to spit it out. And that's what happened. He went too far. He went up to Moscow. And there, the Germans-- the Russians came near Warsaw. The Germans came back. And then the Poles-- that was already summer, '44. And the Warsaw Uprising started. One day suddenly everybody had rifles. Mrs. Stelmachowska had lots of rifles hidden. That's why she was not-- well, she was-- would have been killed for the rifles or for the Jews. She was brave. She didn't mind, whatever it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So are you saying she was working for the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was working for the underground like mad. Later I heard from Wisza and from Ola that she had two Englishmen for a while, and this-- later Wisza met here in London when she visited me, one of this Englishman who was hidden there for a while, who was-- came from a prisoner camp, escaped from a German prisoner camp and came to her. So that-- she was a brave woman. She really-- well, she never lived to get it, but Wisza got this [NON-ENGLISH], the Order of the Righteous People. But in the name of Irene and Wisza Stelmachowska.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now the Warsaw Uprising started. Well, Jan was in seventh heaven. He was suddenly a soldier. You know, he was not the Jew who had to-- not that he said he was a Jew, but he was a Polish soldier, not that he got a rifle or something like that. But he was a messenger between the divisions, or whatever they called it. We didn't [NON-ENGLISH], I never knew when I will see him back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now there was even more hunger at this time. We were in the cellar, because the German bombarded day and night. The Polish-- well, Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brother-in-law, whom I didn't mention before, who was also a biochemist, worked in the underground. He was in the-- in Warsaw, in the underground. He made bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he-- his name was Alfred. He died in the-- in the Warsaw Uprising. So the uprising, day and night bombardments. We had only what we had to eat what we had from before deep in the cellar, and made holes to the next houses, you know, so we had-- we could move if there was one house bombarded. Jan and I went several times for some tomatoes or cabbage, because the gardens around were full of-- it was autumn. Everything was there. But we didn't have it. There were snipers everywhere. We were once nearly killed. It was very, very near.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Poles fought very well, but it was from the beginning, it was not for the-- the Russian were standing on the other side of the Vistula, the river, and looked on. They were very pleased that the Poles were killed. They were very anti-Polish, the Russians. So when on the-- I think the first of October, the-- '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: The truce was signed by the-- it suddenly was quiet. You can't-- can't imagine what it was suddenly quiet after all that bombardment. It was the first time, I think, minesweepers were-- we called it [NON-ENGLISH]. That means cows, because the sound of it was like, what is the sound of a cow? I don't know. But it was like that. We called it [NON-ENGLISH], and that were mines which they fired from far away. That was the end of the Polish Uprising. That's the end of--
